{"step_by_step_thinking": "The question asks which doctor is not authorized to declare brainstem death according to the Transplantation of Human Organs Act. However, one document (Document [11] in the UMLS section) states: 'The Institute of Medicine - American National Academy of Sciences clarified that a clinician could declare death using either neurologic criteria or circulatory criteria.' This implies that neurologically based death (such as brainstem death) can be declared by a qualified clinician. Among the options, a neurosurgeon (C) is a specialist trained in neurological conditions and is likely qualified to declare brainstem death. A treating physician (B) and a surgeon doing a liver transplant (D) may also be qualified depending on their training and institutional protocols. However, the RMP (Resident Medical Officer in charge of the hospital) may not have the specific training or authority to declare brainstem death, especially if such a declaration requires specialized neurological expertise. Since the Transplantation of Human Organs Act typically requires a qualified medical professional with expertise in neurology or critical care, and the RMP is not explicitly mentioned as having such authority, the most reasonable inference is that the RMP incharge of the hospital is not authorized to declare brainstem death.
